does: This paper introduces Sefz, a semantic fuzzing framework that discovers specification violations in LLM-powered agent skills—situations where benign inputs cause skill implementations to breach their own declared safety rules and guardrails. The core contribution is translating natural-language guardrails into reachability goals over annotated execution traces, converting violation detection into deterministic graph queries. Sefz uses an LLM-based mutator to generate inputs whose execution traces progressively approach violation patterns through guided exploration, enabling systematic discovery of these hidden failures that static analysis, traditional fuzzing, and prompt-injection defenses cannot detect. The framework treats the problem as finding inputs that innocuously trigger unsafe behaviors rather than requiring explicit adversarial attacks.
connects: This work extends semantic testing and fuzzing traditions to the domain of LLM agent reliability, building on prior research in specification-based testing and metamorphic testing. It responds to growing concerns about silent failures in AI systems and connects to work on LLM safety, agent robustness, and specification enforcement. The paper addresses gaps left by existing defenses that focus on prompt injection and adversarial attacks while overlooking benign-input violations. It combines execution trace analysis techniques from program verification with LLM-guided search strategies, drawing on mutational fuzzing and goal-directed testing. The focus on guardrail semantics relates to broader literature on translating natural language specifications into executable constraints and monitoring agent behavior against declared policies.
enables: This work establishes foundations for automated specification compliance checking in autonomous agent systems, opening pathways toward building more trustworthy deployments of LLM-powered tools. It enables future work on semantically-aware skill composition, where agents can verify that installed skills respect declared constraints before execution. The approach points toward automated guardrail synthesis and specification repair—automatically patching implementations that violate their documentation. It enables better skill marketplaces and safety auditing infrastructure where third-party skills can be vetted for specification violations before user adoption. The framework could extend to formal specification languages and contract-based agent design, supporting richer safety guarantees for autonomous systems handling critical operations like finance and healthcare.
